{"step_by_step_thinking": "Sealants are primarily used as a preventive and arresting measure for early carious lesions. Multiple documents confirm that sealants are effective in preventing the initiation of caries and arresting noncavitated lesions. For example, Document [11] states that sealants are effective in preventing caries and arresting early noncavitated lesions. Document [12] supports this by noting that sealants can arrest noncavitated and microcavitated lesions. Document [3] and [4] emphasize that initial carious lesions (such as white spot lesions or enamel proximal lesions) can be managed conservatively with sealants, and that remineralization and sealing are preferred over restorations. In contrast, secondary caries (new caries at the margin of a restoration) and recurrent caries are typically associated with failure of existing restorations and are not preventable by sealants. Rampant caries, which is a severe, widespread form of caries, is beyond the scope of sealant effectiveness. Therefore, the most accurate and supported answer is initial caries.", "answer_choice": "A"}
